aliases: Rice with Peanut Sauce and Vegetables

english: Rice with Peanut Sauce and Vegetables

region_of_origin: Indonesia (Java); popular in Johor, Malaysia and Singapore
food_subgroup: Rice-based mixed dishes with vegetables and sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Nasi Jenganan or Nasi Pecel. Mapped to the broader 'rice dish' class (FOODON_00002809) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific code for Nasi Jenganan or Nasi Pecel. Code A00GG (rice-based mixed dish) used as the closest defensible broader category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for Nasi Jenganan or Nasi Pecel as of 2026.

preparation_method: peanut sauce preparation (fried peanuts ground with dried chilies, palm sugar, kaffir lime leaves)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Nasi Jenganan is approximately 350 g, comprising a mound of steamed white rice topped with blanched vegetables, fried tofu and/or tempeh, and a generous ladle of peanut sauce.

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia — composite estimate for rice-based mixed vegetable dish with peanut sauce; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entries for steamed white rice (SR Legacy #20045), boiled kangkung, bean sprouts, long beans, fried tofu, tempeh, and peanut sauce components
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Nasi Jenganan or Nasi Pecel as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA component data and USDA FoodData Central entries for the constituent ingredients (steamed white rice, blanched kangkung, bean sprouts, long beans, fried firm tofu, tempeh, peanut sauce). The input AI-generated values (confidence 0.9) were used as a starting reference and cross-checked against component-level data; they are consistent with the expected nutritional profile of this dish. Values are presented as estimates with medium confidence.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on 'rice-based mixed dish with legumes and vegetables' and 'white rice, boiled' (GI ~64–72 for plain rice, moderated to ~55 by high-fibre vegetables, protein from tofu/tempeh, and fat from peanut sauce)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Nasi Jenganan or Nasi Pecel exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate: plain boiled white rice has a GI of approximately 64–72 (University of Sydney GI Database); the addition of high-fibre blanched vegetables (kangkung, bean sprouts, long beans), protein-rich tofu and tempeh, and fat-rich peanut sauce is well-documented to lower the effective GI of a rice-based meal to the low range (~50–58). GL per serving (350 g) calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × (18.5 × 3.5)) / 100 = 35.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from peanut sauce and added salt (220 mg per 100 g; ~770 mg per 350 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt peanut sauce and limit portion size to control sodium intake, while benefiting from the dish's potassium (280 mg/100 g) and magnesium (42 mg/100 g) content from vegetables and peanuts, which support blood pressure regulation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load (18.5 g/100 g; ~64.75 g per 350 g serving) with low estimated GI (~55) moderated by dietary fibre, protein, and fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes can include Nasi Jenganan in a meal plan by controlling rice portion size and limiting the quantity of peanut sauce, which contains palm sugar; the high fibre from blanched vegetables and protein from tofu and tempeh help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Predominantly unsaturated fat profile from peanuts (estimated saturated fat ~1.2 g/100 g); moderate sodium (220 mg/100 g); beneficial micronutrients including vitamin E (2.2 mg/100 g) and potassium (280 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Nasi Jenganan is relatively heart-friendly due to its unsaturated fat profile from peanuts and soy products, but individuals with cardiovascular disease should monitor sodium from the peanut sauce and overall caloric density; choosing a smaller portion and requesting less sauce reduces cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasi Jenganan is a traditional Javanese-Malay rice dish originating from Java, Indonesia, and particularly popular in the state of Johor, Malaysia, and among the Javanese diaspora in Singapore. It is closely related to Nasi Pecel (its Indonesian name) and shares similarities with Gado-Gado. The dish consists of a serving of steamed white rice topped with an assortment of blanched vegetables — typically kangkung (water spinach), bean sprouts, and long beans — accompanied by fried firm tofu, fried tempeh, and optionally a hard-boiled egg. The defining element is the 'Sambal Jenganan', a rich, thick, velvety peanut sauce made from fried peanuts ground with dried chilies, palm sugar, and aromatic kaffir lime leaves, giving the dish its characteristic nutty, sweet-savory, and mildly spicy flavor. Nutritionally, Nasi Jenganan provides a balanced macronutrient profile per 100 g: approximately 165 kcal, 6.2 g protein, 7.5 g fat (predominantly unsaturated from peanuts), and 18.5 g carbohydrate with 2.4 g dietary fibre. The dish is a good source of potassium (280 mg), magnesium (42 mg), iron (1.5 mg), vitamin E (2.2 mg), and folate (45 mcg) per 100 g. Its estimated glycemic index of approximately 55 (low range) reflects the moderating effect of fibre-rich vegetables, protein from tofu and tempeh, and fat from peanut sauce on the glycemic response of the white rice base. Clinically, it is suitable for most dietary patterns but requires sodium and portion monitoring for individuals with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. It is naturally vegetarian, vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free, making it broadly accessible across dietary requirements.
common_user_queries: Is Nasi Jenganan the same as Nasi Pecel?
How many calories in Nasi Jenganan?
Is Nasi Jenganan suitable for diabetics?
What is the peanut sauce in Nasi Jenganan called?
What vegetables are in Nasi Jenganan?
What is the glycemic index of Nasi Jenganan?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Nasi Jenganan or Nasi Pecel as a composite dish in FAO/INFOODS SEA, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, HPB Singapore, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from component-level data across FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA FoodData Central, cross-checked against the AI-generated input values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.9). The GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database methodology. Medium confidence is appropriate given the absence of a direct composite-dish measurement in any approved source.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-level estimates derived from FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA FoodData Central; no single approved-source entry for Nasi Jenganan or Nasi Pecel as a composite dish was found. The AI-generated input values were used as a reference and are consistent with component-level cross-checks. 2. saturated_fat_g (1.2 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (3.1 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (2.5 g), trans_fat_g (0.0 g), and cholesterol_mg (15.0 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for peanuts, tofu, tempeh, and vegetable oil used in frying; these are not directly reported in the input. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae (60.0 mcg RAE) is converted from the input value of 120 mcg — reported as 60 mcg RAE to align with standard RAE reporting; the input figure of 120 mcg likely reflects a mixed carotenoid/retinol source from vegetables. 4. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA FoodData Central as a single entry. 5. Glycemic load per serving (350 g) = 35.6. 6. The dish is typically vegan/vegetarian but may include a hard-boiled egg as optional; dietary flags reflect the base vegan preparation. 7. Ontology codes use broader-class mappings as no specific codes exist for Nasi Jenganan or Nasi Pecel in FoodOn or FoodEx2 as of 2026. 8. This dish is Indonesian in origin and is also popular in Malaysia and Singapore. Per G6 multilingual rules for Indonesian dishes, the Indonesian local name (Nasi Pecel) is mandatory and is present. Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names and aliases have been added to support retrieval by the Singaporean and Malaysian diaspora communities who consume this dish. Chinese names (simplified: 椰汁花生酱拌饭; traditional: 椰汁花生醬拌飯) are descriptive transliterations meaning 'peanut sauce mixed rice'. Tamil (நாசி ஜெங்கனான்) and Hindi (नासी जेंगनान) are phonetic transliterations of the Malay/Indonesian name. 9. Aliases self-check: every string in aliases_by_language verified to exist verbatim in flat aliases array — PASSED.
